1: The Beautiful Gate (horaia pyle) is not directly identified in first-century sources. The Mishnah (m. Middot Acts 2:3) describes the Nicanor Gate between the Court of Women and the Court of Israel as exceptional in bronze and size; many scholars identify this with Luke's 'Beautiful Gate'. It was the main thoroughfare for Jewish worshippers proceeding inward — a highly visible location for a beggar dependent on the alms-giving piety of pilgrims. The ninth-hour prayer (3:00 pm) was the afternoon Tamid sacrifice, drawing maximum temple traffic.
17: Peter's 'you acted in ignorance' (kata agnoian epraxate) corresponds to the Torah's distinction between inadvertent (bishegagah) and high-handed (beyad ramah) sins (Numbers 15:27-31; Leviticus 5:15-19). Inadvertent sins had sacrificial remedy; deliberate high-handed sins did not. Peter's appeal gives his audience a path: the crucifixion, done in ignorance, can be covered by the same mercy that covers inadvertent sin — if they now respond to the revelation of who Jesus was.
2: The man lame from birth (chōlos ek koilias mētros autou) had been carried to the Beautiful Gate daily. Congenital lameness was incurable by ancient medicine; his forty-plus years of age (Acts 3:22 implies the duration of the condition) is emphasized in Acts 3:22 to establish the miracle's completeness. Professional beggars positioned themselves at temple gates because alms-giving to the poor was a Torah obligation (Deuteronomy 15:7-11) — temple-goers were primed for generosity. The Beautiful Gate's high traffic made it the optimal location.
3: The man asked for alms (ērōta eleēmosunēn labein): eleēmosunē (alms) is the material expression of eleos (mercy/steadfast love). In Jewish practice, alms-giving was one of the three pillars of piety alongside prayer and fasting (Matthew 6:2-4; Tob Acts 12:8-9). The high priest's household may have been exempt from giving to beggars (since all temple revenue was theoretically already consecrated), but individual worshippers were expected to give. Peter and John's poverty ('silver and gold I do not have') reflects genuine economic reality.
4: 'Peter looked directly at him, as did John, and said, Look at us' (atenisas eis auton... blepson eis hēmas): the sustained direct eye contact before miraculous action is a feature of both prophetic and Hellenistic healing narratives (the healer's eyes focusing attention). The command to look is also a demand for undivided attention to what is about to happen — the healing will not be performed on an inattentive recipient. Luke's detail establishes the personal, focused character of apostolic healing distinct from magical formulae.
5: The man gives them his full attention (epieichen autois, prosdokōn ti par autōn labein) expecting to receive money. His expectation is material — he does not ask for healing (he may not have known it was possible). The gap between what the man expects (coins) and what he receives (restored legs) is Luke's irony: the greater gift arrives disguised as a refusal of the lesser. This pattern recurs throughout Luke-Acts: divine provision exceeds human asking.
6: 'In the name of Jesus Christ of the Nazarene, walk' (en tō onomati Iēsou Christou tou Nazōraiou, peripatei): the name as the vehicle of healing power establishes that Peter is an agent, not the source. The Nazarene title (Nazōraios) is the precise identification used at the trial and crucifixion (John 18:5, 7; Acts 19:19); Peter uses the name the authorities had inscribed on the cross as the healing power. The irony is compressed: Rome's label of execution becomes the apostles' invocation of life.
7: Peter took him by the right hand and raised him up (piasas auton... ēgeiren auton): egeirō (raise up) is the resurrection verb throughout the NT. The physical action — taking the right hand and lifting — mirrors the gesture Paul used for Eutychus (Acts 20:10, who fell from the window) and Elijah/Elisha for the dead children they raised. Luke's use of the resurrection vocabulary for this healing implicitly connects healing and resurrection as expressions of the same divine life-giving power.
8: 'Jumping up, he stood and began to walk, and he entered the temple walking and jumping and praising God' — the threefold physical response (stand, walk, enter) followed by jumping (exallomenos) and praise reverses the congenital condition with exuberant completeness. The image echoes Isaiah 35:6: 'then the lame will leap like a deer' — one of the restoration-of-creation signs associated with the eschatological era. Luke presents the healing as more than a medical miracle: it is a sign that Isaiah's promised restoration has begun.
9: 'All the people saw him walking and praising God' — the public character of the healing is emphasized: the gate was the most visible point of temple entry, and the man who had been there daily as a well-known fixture was now walking and leaping. The eyewitness public is the community of attestation. Luke's careful establishment of witnesses — this man known to have been lame from birth (Acts 3:10) — is consistent with his historiographical method: the miraculous is not private but publicly verifiable.
10: 'They recognized him as the one who sat at the Beautiful Gate of the temple, asking for alms, and they were filled with wonder and amazement (thambous kai ekstaseōs) at what had happened to him' — thamboa and ekstasin are the standard Lukan reaction to the inexplicable divine act. Ekstasis (standing outside oneself, ecstasy) appears at the healing of the paralytic (Luke 5:26), at the empty tomb (Mark 16:8), and at Peter's Joppa trance (Acts 10:10). The crowd's amazement is credentialing evidence that the healing was genuine and recognized.
11: Solomon's Portico (Stoa tou Solomōnos) was the colonnade along the eastern wall of the outer court (Court of the Gentiles). Josephus (Ant. 20.220-222; Jewish War 5.185) describes it as the only structure surviving from Solomon's time, or as a Herodian reconstruction on older foundations. It was a known gathering place for teachers: Jesus taught there (John 10:23), and the early community met there regularly (Acts 5:12). Its eastern orientation was associated with the rising sun and messianic expectation (Ezekiel 43:1-4).
12: 'Men of Israel, why are you amazed at this? Or why do you stare at us, as if by our own power or godliness we had made him walk?' — Peter's denial of personal credit is the standard prophetic disclaimer (cf. Elisha: 'as the LORD lives, before whom I stand', 2 Kings 5:16). The crowd's amazement is misdirected toward the instruments; Peter redirects to the agent. The two terms — dynamis (power) and eusebeia (godliness/piety) — address both the supernatural and the moral aspects of the crowd's possible misconceptions.
13: 'The God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, the God of our fathers' — the threefold patriarchal formula (Exodus 3:6, 15; spoken at the burning bush) establishes continuity between the God who acts in the healing and the God of Israel's entire history. 'Has glorified his servant Jesus' (edoxasen ton paida autou Iēsoun): pais (servant/child) echoes the Servant-vocabulary of Isaiah 52:13 ('my servant will be exalted and lifted up'). The glorification through resurrection is the direct reversal of the crowd's handing him over.
14: 'You denied the Holy and Righteous One (ton hagion kai dikaion) and asked for a murderer to be granted to you' — the Barabbas reference (Luke 23:18-19). Hagios (holy) and dikaios (righteous/just) are Messianic throne titles with OT weight: tsaddiq (righteous) is the Isaianic Servant's description (Isaiah 53:11). The exchange — the Holy and Righteous One for a murderer — is the moral inversion that defines the crucifixion. Peter's charge is precise: they chose wrongly in the specific legal moment that mattered.
15: 'You killed the Author of life (ton archēgon tēs zōēs apeктeinate), whom God raised from the dead. We are witnesses of this' — archēgos (pioneer, originator, leader) in Acts is used twice for Jesus: here (Acts 3:15) and in Acts 5:31. The word was used for the founder of a city or colony — the first one through the gates who establishes the settlement. Peter's compressed paradox is the sharpest in Acts: they destroyed the source of life, and the source of life defeated their destruction. The witness claim follows immediately: not an abstract theological assertion but personal testimony.
16: 'By faith in his name, his name has made this man strong' — the double onoma (name) and double pistis (faith) in this verse are unusual; the syntax is compressed almost to the point of opacity. The onoma here carries the full Semitic weight: the name is the power and person concentrated. The faith is not the lame man's (he expected money, not healing) but the faith of those who invoked the name — or the faith the name itself generates (hē pistis hē di autou: the faith through him). Either reading credits Christ as the healing agent.
18: 'What God foretold by the mouth of all the prophets, that his Christ would suffer, he thus fulfilled' — the claim that 'all the prophets' predicted the Messiah's suffering is a hermeneutical claim about the whole OT, not a list of specific proof-texts. First-century Judaism generally did not read the suffering Servant texts (Isa 53) or the royal lament psalms (Ps 22, 69) as pointing to a suffering Messiah; the dominant expectation was of a victorious king. Peter's pesher — reading the whole prophetic tradition as a network pointing to the Messiah's passion — represents the early church's major interpretive contribution.
19: 'Repent therefore and turn back (epistrepsate), that your sins may be blotted out, that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord' — epistrephō (turn/return) is the LXX equivalent of Hebrew shub, the covenant-return vocabulary of the prophets (Hosea 6:1; Jeremiah 3:12-14; Zechariah 1:3). Anapsyxis (refreshing) is a hapax in the NT — a rare word suggesting renewal after exhaustion (cf. Exodus 23:12 LXX: 'on the seventh day you shall rest and be refreshed'). The refreshing is both personal (forgiveness) and cosmic (the eschatological restoration of Acts 3:21).
20: 'That he may send the Christ appointed for you, Jesus' (aposteilē ton prokecheirismenon hymin Christon Iēsoun): prokecheirismenon (appointed/designated beforehand) uses the same verb as Paul's Damascus commission (Acts 22:14; Acts 26:16). Jesus is the pre-appointed Christ who has already come and will be sent again. The parousia here is conditional on Israel's repentance — a theological claim that has generated debate about whether national repentance accelerates the end-time (cf. Romans 11:25-26). The sending language echoes the prophetic tradition of divine envoys sent repeatedly to Israel.
21: 'Heaven must receive until the time of restoration of all things' (apokatastaseōs pantōn): apokatastasis (restoration to original condition) appears only here in the NT in this specific phrase. In Jewish tradition it referred to the end-time renewal when all things would be set right (the ingathering of exiles, cosmic restoration, the defeat of evil). Origen later used this term for universal salvation, but here the context is the OT prophetic vision — everything spoken through the holy prophets from old being restored to its proper order.
22: Deuteronomy 18:15 cited: 'Moses said, The Lord God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers. You shall listen to him in everything he tells you' — the Mosaic prophet-expectation was a major Second Temple hope. 1 Macc 4:46 and 14:41 defer certain decisions 'until a prophet should arise'; Qumran expected 'the prophet' alongside the priestly and royal Messiahs (1QS Acts 9:11). Peter identifies Jesus as this prophet. The command 'listen to him' echoes the Transfiguration voice (Luke 9:35: 'hear him'), connecting the prophetic commission to that scene.
23: 'Every soul who does not listen to that prophet shall be destroyed from the people' (Deuteronomy 18:19; Leviticus 23:29) — the consequence of rejecting Moses's appointed successor is excision from the covenant community (exolethreuthēsetai ek tou laou). Peter applies this to his audience's response to Jesus: rejecting Jesus is rejecting the prophet Moses appointed, which carries the Torah's own threatened consequence. The warning is not ethnic judgment against Israel but a covenant-faithfulness appeal: the same Torah that established Israel demands loyalty to the one Moses foretold.
24: 'And all the prophets who have spoken, from Samuel and those who came after him, also proclaimed these days' — Samuel is the beginning of the classical prophetic tradition; the inclusion of 'all the prophets from Samuel' encompasses the entire prophetic canon from the period of the monarchy. Peter is arguing for a continuous prophetic consensus: the days of Messiah's coming have been the subject of all genuine prophetic speech since Israel's prophetic office began. This totalizing claim about prophetic Scripture is the basis for the Christian OT.
25: 'You are the sons of the prophets and of the covenant that God made with your fathers, saying to Abraham, And in your offspring shall all the families of the earth be blessed' (Genesis 22:18; Acts 12:3) — the Abrahamic covenant is the theological warrant for the Gentile mission already embedded in Peter's speech to a Jewish audience. Huioi... tēs diathēkēs (sons of the covenant) is a Semitism for covenant-members. The quotation of Genesis 22:18 in en tō spermati sou (in your seed/offspring) is the same text Paul exegetes in Galatians 3:16 as referring to Christ.
26: 'God, having raised up his servant, sent him to you first, to bless you by turning each of you from your wickedness' (en tō apostrephein hekaston apo tōn ponēriōn hymōn): the servant language (pais) echoes Isaiah 52:13 again. The 'first' (prōton) is the Pauline priority of Romans 1:16: 'to the Jew first and also to the Greek'. The blessing of the nations through Abraham's seed — just quoted — begins with Israel's own conversion. The sequence first-to-Israel then-to-the-nations is built into the Abrahamic covenant's own structure.
